title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Edna informs Colonel Johnny Samoyski and Mrs. Sweetman that she witnessed a woman entering a house between 8:30 and 9:00 p.m. while Edna was watching from the shadows of a tree across the street. Edna describes the woman as having golden hair and wearing a black coat, noting this distinguishes her from Dierdre Henderson, who has dark hair. Edna observed the woman enter the house while the porch light was on, mentioning that Mr. Robbins and a female novelist were at the theater at the time.

Distressed by the prospect of her strict father's reaction, Edna becomes increasingly upset. Mrs. Sweetman reveals to Samoyski that Edna had been lying about her whereabouts; rather than going to the Keller place to see Reggie, Edna had been waiting to meet Charlie Masterson, a married farmhand from the Colvin place. To protect Edna's reputation, Mrs. Sweetman arranges for her to be driven discreetly to the police station in Gillingchester via Samson's pickup truck.

In Gillingchester, Hercule Poirot meets with Inspector Spence. Poirot expresses his distaste for the victim, James Benthall, whom he views as self-centered and ungrateful. Poirot reports that Benthall provided little useful information, though he mentioned that Mrs. McGinty claimed someone related to the "Craig case" lives in Broadhurst Gardens and would be "proud" if the truth were known. Spence notes that investigating local backgrounds is difficult due to the transient nature of the population and the destruction of records caused by the war, specifically mentioning families such as the Samoys and the Carpenters.
